items.front: What operational reason does the 3-1-1 note give for why traffic information is especially helpful around terminals?
items.back: Because of the limited space around terminal locations.

items.front: What two groups of aircraft does the 3-1-1 note say traffic information can help pilots avoid collisions between?
items.back: Aircraft operating within Class B/C/D surface areas and terminal radar service areas, and transiting aircraft operating in proximity to terminal locations.

items.front: Does traffic information under 3-1-1 transfer, share, or remove the pilot's collision-avoidance responsibility?
items.back: No. The pilot remains responsible for collision avoidance. Traffic information aids but does not shift the responsibility.

items.front: Why does the abnormality phraseology use 'APPEARS' rather than a definitive mechanical statement?
items.back: The controller reports what is visually observed, not a mechanical diagnosis. 'Appears' acknowledges the controller is not certifying the aircraft's condition.

items.front: What are the two triggers in 3-1-10 for informing an aircraft of an observed abnormal condition?
items.back: 1) When requested by a pilot. 2) When the controller deems it necessary.

items.front: What prerequisite must be met before approving a pilot's request to cross a Class C or D surface area or exceed the speed limit?
items.back: Traffic conditions must permit.

items.front: What two operational consequences of unusual maneuvers does the 3-1-11 note list?
items.back: They increase hazards to persons and property, and they contribute to noise complaints.

items.front: Which of these is NOT listed as an unusual maneuver in 3-1-11: unnecessary low passes, unscheduled flybys, practice instrument approaches below minima, touch-and-goes, buzz jobs?
items.back: Touch-and-goes (unless a landing or touch-and-go is to be made is an exception to the practice-approach rule, not a prohibited maneuver).

items.front: What is the qualitative standard for local controller runway scanning in 3-1-12?
items.back: To the maximum extent possible.

items.front: When does 3-1-12 say ground control's scanning assistance is especially important?
items.back: When runways are in close proximity to other movement areas.

items.front: What critical operational difference distinguishes '(call sign) standby' from '(call sign) REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY'?
items.back: Both establish communications, but the second adds an explicit instruction keeping the aircraft outside the Delta until services can be provided. The first alone allows entry.

items.front: What is the condition that triggers the REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE phraseology?
items.back: Workload or traffic conditions prevent immediate provision of airport traffic control services.

items.front: What qualifying phrase applies to both the taxi restriction and rolling takeoff requirement in 3-1-14?
items.back: "To the extent possible."

items.front: What two operational harms does newly airborne volcanic ash cause to following aircraft?
items.back: Significantly reduced visibility and engine ingestion.

items.front: What are the three phraseology options available under 3-1-15 for responding to a Tarmac Rule return request?
items.back: 1) TAXI TO (ramp/gate/deplaning area) VIA (route). 2) EXPECT A (number) MINUTE DELAY DUE TO (traffic type) TRAFFIC. 3) UNABLE DUE TO OPERATIONAL DISRUPTION.

items.front: Where are the significant-disruption criteria for the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule defined, and how do they vary?
items.back: In the facility directive. They vary by airport.

items.front: What is the defining operational difference between preventive control and normal airport traffic control?
items.back: Repetitious, routine approval of pilot action is eliminated. Controllers intervene only when they observe a traffic conflict developing.

items.front: What DoD-specific documents can satisfy the LOA requirement for preventive control under 3-1-2?
items.back: Airfield Operating instructions, Memorandums of Understanding, or other specific directives used exclusively by the Department of Defense.

items.front: What specific information must be included in the coordination between ground and local before a runway crossing?
items.back: The point/intersection at the runway where the operation will occur.

items.front: What must the ground controller do after a coordinated runway crossing operation is complete?
items.back: Advise the local controller that the operation is complete, either verbally or through visual aids as specified by the facility directive.

items.front: How are exceptions to the positive coordination requirements of 3-1-3 authorized?
items.back: Only through paragraph 1-1-11 waivers and JO 7210.3 paragraph 10-1-7, where justified by extraordinary circumstances at specific locations.

items.front: What is the minimum information that must be exchanged between local and ground controllers under 3-1-4?
items.back: Aircraft identification and applicable runway/intersection/taxiway information.

items.front: What special coordination does 3-1-4c require when runways or aircraft are not visible from the tower or on radar?
items.back: Advise the local/ground controller of the aircraft's location before releasing the aircraft to the other controller.

items.front: What three operational conditions does 3-1-5a list during which LOA-covered vehicles may operate in the RSA?
items.back: When aircraft are arriving, departing, or taxiing along the runway.

items.front: What is the specific scope limitation for 'PROCEED AS REQUESTED' phraseology under 3-1-5?
items.back: It is not approved for instructing aircraft, vehicles, equipment, or personnel to cross or operate on a runway. It is for RSA operations up to the runway edge under an LOA.

items.front: What is the operational focus difference between 3-1-6 subparagraph a and subparagraph b?
items.back: Subpara a: describing vehicles, equipment, or personnel on or near the movement area (non-aircraft hazards). Subpara b: describing the relative position of other aircraft (traffic) to the receiving aircraft.

items.front: What paragraph governs the radar traffic advisory phraseology that may be used with a CTRD under 3-1-6c?
items.back: Paragraph 2-1-21, Traffic Advisories.

items.front: What are the three methods for determining position under 3-1-7?
items.back: 1) Visually. 2) Through a display system. 3) Via position reports (when visual and display methods are unavailable).

items.front: What three actions require prior position determination under 3-1-7?
items.back: Issuing taxi instructions, issuing takeoff clearance, and authorizing personnel/equipment onto the movement area.

items.front: How long must wind shear/microburst information remain on the ATIS after the last report or indication?
items.back: 20 minutes.

items.front: What is the detection limitation of LLWAS?
items.back: It detects low level wind shear only around the periphery of an airport. It does not detect wind shear beyond that limitation.

items.front: Under what sensor-failure condition is LLWAS NE++/LLWAS−RS considered out of service for a specific runway?
items.back: When all three remote sensors designated for that runway's arrival or departure wind display line are inoperative. (The system can operate with up to 50% of total sensors inoperative.)

items.front: When does separation responsibility resume after a departure aircraft executes a wind shear escape maneuver?
items.back: When the crew informs ATC the escape is complete AND EITHER ATC observes approved separation re-established OR the crew has resumed the previously assigned departure clearance.

items.front: During a wind shear escape, what should the controller NOT do with respect to the escape aircraft?
items.back: Issue control instructions that are contrary to the pilot's escape actions.

items.front: What services may NOT be provided using uncertified tower display workstations?
items.back: Radar services and traffic advisories. Only general position information in easy-to-understand terms may be given.

items.front: Why should pilots not be told they are in 'radar contact' when working with a tower radar display?
items.back: Tower radar functions are not considered radar services. Local controllers cannot assure continued positive radar identification per paragraph 5-3-1 without creating operationally inefficient and distracting requirements.

items.front: What must local controllers at nonapproach control towers devote the majority of their time to?
items.back: Visually scanning the runways and local area.
